summary:
Nero (r. 54–68 AD) was the fifth Roman emperor and the last of the Julio-Claudian dynasty — a figure whose reign began promisingly (the 'Quinquennium Neronis,' his first five years of good governance under Seneca and Burrus) but descended into tyranny, paranoia, and eventually the first civil war since Augustus. His name became synonymous with imperial viciousness: he executed his own mother Agrippina the Younger, had his wife Octavia killed and replaced with Poppaea Sabina (whom he later allegedly kicked to death), and executed his advisor Seneca.

Nero's more colorful characteristics — his obsession with artistic performance, his insistence on appearing on stage as an actor and musician (deeply shameful for a Roman aristocrat), his participation in the Olympic Games (where he was declared winner despite falling from his chariot), and his general contempt for Roman senatorial dignity — contributed to the aristocratic detestation that has shaped his historical image. His administration of the empire was not uniformly catastrophic: the [[event:2017|Great Fire of Rome]]'s rebuilding program genuinely improved the city, and his finances, though strained, were managed. The [[event:2822|Great Persecution Diocletian]]'s later persecutions were far more systematic, but Nero initiated the tradition of Roman persecution of Christians. His suicide in 68 AD ('What an artist dies in me!') ended the dynasty and triggered the [[event:2814|Year Four Emperors]] Year of the Four Emperors.
